titleStmt.title: Letter from Fr Francis M. Shaw to Fr Provincial Thomas V. Nolan, 1 August 1918
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Francis Shaw

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from Francis M. Shaw (1881-1924) to the Father Provincial Thomas V Nolan (1867-1941) regarding the weather and a meeting with Father Garold and Father Molony. Francis, or Frank, Shaw SJ was born in Ennis, county Clare. He was ordained on 31 July 1916 at Milltown Park, Dublin. Fr Shaw joined the war early in 1916, working in the 16th General Field Hospital at Le Tréporte. In 1917, possibly due to his nationalistic views, Fr Shaw was dispatched to India, and later to Mesopotamia.Fr. Thomas V. Nolan was the Provincial of the Irish Province of the Society of Jesus (1912-1922) and was a member of the Distribution Committee which looked after the welfare and distribution of the Belgian refugees who arrived in Ireland as a consequence of the First World War.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
P.S. when the Poona
Pay Dept. have fixed up
my financial affairs for
period March â December 1917
I shall have a substantial
balance to forward to
Your Reverence.
FS. Mesopotamia 1 August 1918. Dear Reverend Father Provincial, P.C. A letter from your Reverence and a card
from Fr Farley to hand last week. I am still going strong, and
not much inconvenienced by heat, though it is hot enough. A Father
Watson C.S.S.R. died on the Euphrates of heat stroke last week. August
will see the end ofthe really high temperatures . But there are various
other possibilities outside the weather. Last night Fr Garrold S.J. , ofthe English Province, and I, entertained
our senior Chaplain, Fr Moloney, to dinner in Baghdad, in honour
ofSt.Ignatius. We had no status to read; but we both wished
we could hear ourselves consigned to some place like Galway or
St. Beuno's. However, I suppose that happy consummation will come
some day. On Sunday I did a hot tramp ofa couple ofmilestosay Mass.
On arrival at the place I found I had left the missel behind.
I said the Rosary, and then tramped back and said Mass privately.
Memory is an uncertain quantity out here. I tried to write out the
proper ofthe Mass which I recite by heart most ofthe time, but
of course I came on sudden blanks, which wouldn't fill up no
matter how often I 'went back and took a run at them'. During July I said 17 Masses for Fr Farley. I should have had more
only I lost four mornings travelling up river, and said Masses for
Fr Watson, two Catholic Officers killed in Trench Mortar accident, and
a cholera patient who is now on the way back after a strenuous attempt
to get out oftheworld. A Catholic Times, Irish Catholic, and Weekly Freeman have reached
me. Many thanks towhoever sent them. I recommend myself to the prayers of all. Francis M.Shaw S.J.
